speaker: interviewer
text: --interview with Eva Weiss, November 16, 1995, tape number 3.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Weiss, tell me about what life was like in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: Life in a ghetto was also-- well, that's-- already, we faced up to it, that something terrible is happening to us. And they're going to ship the Jews out of their country, and never be no more Jews in Hungary, and all that. And we-- you know, and then my father heard the stories. And now they started to believe that this is must've been how it was in Poland, and how it was in Czechoslovakia, and all over in Russia. And at the-- at the ghetto, when we went into the building, we-- family, and even so, we knew all the people in Olaszliszka, all the Jews-- sort of all wanted to stick together. So my uncle, my aunt, and three girls-- four girls, and a son, was with the same building, same room with us. And, if I remember correctly, my father-- [SIGHS] the men went to take the next bedroom. And all the women, we were sleeping together on the floor, no bed. Just-- and I think that we must have took some pillows, or, or their pillows, a blanket. Anyway, we're laying in bed. And the two women-- no, three, because our next-door neighbor we took with us to there because their daughter escaped. And I'd like to chase back a little bit how this particular next-door neighbor came over to us, just before we go into the synagogue and gather the Jews, that his three daughters, with, with gentile paper, escaping from going to anywhere we go, ghetto or what-- and came over, middle of the night Saturday night, to my father. He knocks on a-- on a window from the roof. Somehow he climbed over. And he was an older man-- and begged my father to let my sister come. Money was no object. Because, he says, I'll manage that Edith will have a gentile paper. And let the girls escape. And money was, for him-- because of black market, he had extra money, wherever he hid it. He would have paid. And he would have, you know, be able to do it. And, like I said, they always worried about the grown girls. And my sister was, at that point, 21, 22-- beautiful girl. She was actually engaged to a boy who was taken to Ukraine and never came back, either. And my father and my mother was, you know, so weak. And my sister just couldn't leave them. And, you know, she would maybe go if my father forced her. But my father says, no. If we have to die, we die together.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, let's, let's go back to the ghetto. If-- did you have, did you have-- get rations in the ghetto and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, well, the ration was, from all us all over-- our people, the Jewish family who took in the food-- like, three family, whatever they had with, they put it together. And we divided it, evenly-- food. So three days later, they realize, there's no food given out.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they-- a gendarme and a German soldier appears. And they're asking my father name. And, of course, when they called you, you know, right away, you get scared. My mother is jittering. What did daddy do? Maybe he-- dared something to do, and he gets punished for it.

speaker: interviewee
text: They ask him and two other Jewish men to go back to Liska with the wagon, and take a look, and confiscate all the food what they've hidden away and bring it back. Because they realized that we have no food. So sure enough, my father goes back to Liska-- and with the other one. And they loaded up, I think, about two or three wagons-- flour, schmaltz, smoked meat, if they find a little bit left, whatever. And canned-- we didn't have canned goods. So we preserve it in jars. And the jar food, anything it was. And then, when they brought it all in, they made, like, a community kitchen. And they rationed out the flour and things like that. And this way, they were able to do it. But the ration was so limited. I remember that my sister was helping. And the older girls, the cousins and my mother, they all in a kitchen, trying to make soup with a little bit of vegetables, or whatever they had. And at one point, I remember coming-- a package arrived. And they give it to us, which my uncle from Debrecen send us. And they were goodies like salami, like, you know, regular [NON-ENGLISH] stuff, kolbase, but everything-- and packed. And, of course, we didn't touch it. We just leave it there for a more-- worse time, and to be an emergency.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in a ghetto, you just-- it was like a-- where we were, it was a courtyard, like four buildings, [POINTING] here, here, here, and a courtyard. We just gathered around, and just talked. I don't know-- and helping to be clean and sanitary, help everybody to wash. And everybody had a chore what to do. OK. How long were you in the ghetto for?

speaker: interviewee
text: Four weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you go from there?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Auschwitz. Then they started to, to deport us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about the trip to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, a trip to Auschwitz, the only thing I remember-- that our building, I think, was the last transport. Was four transport going out of Ujhely. I have no idea how many thousands of Jews it was. Because, you know, we get to the, the train station, which, of course, we had first experienced was from Liska to Ujhely-- the cattle cars. And we go in there. And they shove us into the cattle car. And in that point, too, we try to be as families together. [SIGHS] These family, the Weisses, and us, again, together in a wagon-- I mean, in a cattle car. And we're going in. We had [POINTS] this much space just to cram down on a floor. Put your feet like this-- and crushed in, and shoved in there. We have no idea where we're going. At one point, I remember, they talked about-- and the rumor was that we're going to Madagascar. We didn't even know where it is, someplace in the [LAUGHS] Middle East somewhere, you know. I mean, it was no name of Auschwitz. It was no place. But we knew that we going to go someplace-- Poland or far-away country-- and maybe we're going to work. So the, the trip last us, like, two nights, two days. And again, as a little schlimazel, I am sick. And I have pain and cramps. And I'm crying. My mother is holding my hand. And, and in there, too, before we took off-- and they started to tell us, you know, that you can take two suitcases. So you take the little clothing. You put your winter coat on again. And then you take a few layers of clothes, and food, a little bit of food. The package what I told you that arrived for us, my, my father wouldn't even open it and, and share out nothing. We're taking that along. And my sister gets very hungry and is just, Mother, [NON-ENGLISH], I want it open, this package. And I want to see what's in there. And I want to have a little salami. And my mother says, no, you don't have to eat [NON-ENGLISH] yet. You don't have to. You're not hungry enough. Don't eat [NON-ENGLISH] yet.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us [NON-ENGLISH] means.

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH] means non-kosher. And, and, [LAUGHS] and she won't even let my sister have the piece of salami yet. I'll never forget. So even I'm sick-- my father finally opens a package, a little bundle. And he had a little-- [SIGHS] almost like a little silver, or metal container, which was [NON-ENGLISH] in that, which is a strong whiskey. And he gives me a little shot, just from the bottle, to swallow. And I guess it must've knocked me out. So the rest of the night, I think I'm sleeping. And we go on a train. And it's dark. And it's nighttime. And the men get up, all the men. I have no idea how many of us we were in, in a-- in a cattle car. I know the two family was together, the Weisses and the Lefkovits, and all the cousins, and some, you know, other people, too. And as we're approaching daylight-- so all the men, especially the tall ones, they try to see where we going. And the scenery is spring. And it's green everywhere. You know, this is already in May, a day after [NON-ENGLISH] Day, when we left. And we were the last transport from Ujhely. I know it because my rest of the family went earlier. If they were in a different building, they were in a different line, different transport. And my father looks out. And he says, look at it, look at it, and how beautiful and green. He says, we'll be all right. We're not going to be killed. We're probably going to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this went on, you know. And whatever little food or whatever we had, is rationed. And the sanitary was the worst thing-- the worst thing I remember. I don't even think they were prepared for it. Or was it a pail there or something?

speaker: interviewee
text: Here you are, cattle car. You have no dignity. You have no shame. They-- I have to get up the men. And then as, as-- the girls, I think, we were worse. And the younger, I think, you were-- I, I know how I felt, so shame, so shame. I wouldn't go. I wouldn't change myself, nothing. It was horrible. And two days went like this, that morning, they daven. The men get up. They were praying. And the women try to open up a [NON-ENGLISH] and press out the kids for everybody, the little bit of food what we had. And the day went by. And whoever-- they take turns and alternate to lift up the kids, you know, let them look through a little bit, the scenery, let them see daylight. And a following morning, dawn comes. And this train is slowing down. We don't know anything. We have no idea. I don't know if they ever caught the borders.

speaker: interviewer
text: There were signs. Where are we? Poland or Germany? We have no idea where we are. At least, I didn't know, I know that. And, as the train slows down, my father right away gets up. And they're davening, and all this. And then, you know, daylight breaks. And he says, in, in Jewish, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. He says, thank-- or, or, or how does he say? Thank God, he says, we're all right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we hear music. And my father lifts me up, too. And I see diapers flying, children voice, everything. And everybody laughing or, or, or crying. And they're so happy that, you know, we are not going to be killed. We're together. And we probably going to work, whatever it going to be.

speaker: interviewer
text: So train slows down. And after that, everything was so fast, so organized, Fran, that you haven't got no-- you cannot visualize it. And here we are in a train. Then they open up, that-- you know, sliding open that big gate of the cattle car. And a daylight just hits you after two days being in darkness.

speaker: interviewee
text: And right away, my sister puts her coat on. I put my coat on. And we get dressed and everything, put, put the bundles together. And the chaos and hollering-- raus! Raus! Raus! And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What is 'raus'?

speaker: interviewee
text: 'Raus' means get out, get out. And [SIGHS] train stops. And they say, get out and form a line. And I think they were translating in Hungarian, too. I'm pretty sure. And we form a line-- men here, women here. So they're five men-- my brother-- my, my older brother was 17, Sandor; my father; my uncle, [NON-ENGLISH] Weiss; and his son; and my other-- the neighbor, I think, it was Mr. Klein-- formed five. We had to be five in a row. And next to him-- he was standing right here, next to us-- the older girls were four, and somebody else with them. The Weiss girl, three-- and my sister, who's older. And I am holding my mother's hand, my little brother the other side. My aunt-- my aunt holding her little child, who was Eva, too, but she was about 12 or 13-- and some other. We have to be five, no matter what.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was just 15 minutes. And they hollered, don't bring your suitcase. Leave everything there. Just come off. And they just actually throw us out or grab us and dump us down, you know, from the-- from the cattle car.

speaker: interviewee
text: And right away, rails-- as-- you didn't have time to look. But as you looked, you saw thousands of people coming out of that long train. We have no idea who, what, where. At this point, you just want to be together. That's it. That was the main thing-- holding hands, and the girls, too, holding hands. And [SIGHS] that I remember vividly. As we arriving and are forming a line, the railroad track gate was sort of in front of us, you know, where we were. And a German is front of me. And, of course, he asking everybody how old you are. I definitely would have said 15, which is 15. And all of a sudden, my sister and three Weiss girls, who was about 21, 24, and I think was the oldest, 25, they, they go right there, to the left. And I'm holding my mother's hand and facing that gate. And a soldier, he would, this second, ask me how old I am. But a truck was coming. And for split second, he turns around. I let my mother hand go and run after my sister. Edith, Edith! That instinct was with me that I wanted to be with the big girls always, you know, that I want to grow up, whatever. Don't ask me. And, and I'm holding my mother's hand, other side my little brother who's 13, my father there. And as I run after her, at that second, they could have shot me, right there. But it was just a split second. And as you were going off the track, you know, it was like a hill running down. And I look. And I see the four girls going. And I'm running after them. And then we get there. And we had to form a line.

speaker: interviewer
text: So right away, my sister grabs me. And I'm the fifth in a line. And I don't even know who was next to her. But the three cousins, the Weisses, my sister, and me. And we are five.

speaker: interviewee
text: You look back upon that hill, no father, no mother. I think for one second, we could look back because we were right away, march. As soon as you were in line, you march. You don't know where, but you march. And, and that's it. I never saw her face. I never saw nobody. [CRYING] We get in that line. And then they take us into a horrible, huge [SIGHS] room with white-- I remember that white tile-- white-- and white-and-black tile. I have no idea. It's huge, clean room. And start stripping you off. And there I am, sick again. And my poor sister was so-- and me, embarrassed. And right away, they strip us. Put us-- No, first they stripped, and naked, they make you sit while they shave your hair. We get off the chair. I, I, I recognize my sister from the bust. I couldn't recognize her face. Gorgeous black hair up to here, beautiful girl. And we look at each other, like, crazy. I mean, this was so fast and so organized that I cannot tell you. And, of course, you see women soldier. They were women soldier, German Aufseherinnen, they called them. Soldier run and hollering. And some already probably talking Hungarian, somebody in there. And we're lining up. And then our main concern-- to stick together. So when one sit on a chair, I think my sister stood there till I get off that chair, all shaven, naked, standing. And then you went into a shower. So I'm coming out of the shower. At least we washed. And they threw us-- [SIGHS] I had a green, like, a crepe dress to my ankle. I mean, in the showers, we started laughing because we looked so hideous, all of us, that, that I-- it's unspeakable. You couldn't-- you couldn't even imagine. And hundreds and hundreds of women, small, short, young, old, all naked, and all shaven-- nobody knew nothing. But we sort of cling together. And we watched. Then again, when you form a line, that's a five-- it's going to be. So that's how it was. So we were shaven. And then we were taken in an huge barracks. And we stayed there overnight. I don't know. I think it was called Birkenau. I don't even know where we were. The next morning, dawn came. 5 o'clock, we have to be out. They're counting us. Again, form a line with five of us, with the clothing what they give us. And, and then they give us that little, like the army's guy has, you know, the little kettle for the food. And they give you a spoon and fork attached to it, from metal, whatever. And we form a line. And we're going to get breakfast. At the breakfast, which is 6:00

speaker: interviewee
text: 00 in the morning, we have no idea. It was, I think, 5:00 in the morning and cool, because it was, you know, early May. And they're serving us. First time in my life saw a big kettle like this. And the, the food was, I mean, disgusting. It was-- actually, we knew after that what was it. Beets what you feed the cows with, big beets in big, thick slices, boiled-- and that's it, something. And as we stay in line, who is beside the other side is one of my cousin from Olaszliszka, who all went a day before us to Auschwitz. She already has a tiny little babushka covered her bare head. And she's serving.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when she sees me, she comes out of there, hugs me, says Evika, what you doing here? She says, listen to me. From this day on, you're 18 years old. And from that day on-- she told me right away because she spoke pretty good German-- and taught me that you're 18 years old, [GERMAN] year old.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's how I register. So when they take the dates from then on, I was born in, you know, 1926, what, or something. And the date I gave them, obvious the right date, December 10.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever see your parents or your brother again?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. My brother, just after liberation. No, no, no, nobody. Nobody. We were-- we have five cousin all the way through the end. And two of us came back.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in Auschwitz for? And what--

speaker: interviewee
text: That, that day, we were only day, one day.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, where did you go from there?

speaker: interviewee
text: From that day again, started to, you know, take us back to the, the train station. I'm sure it was a different. And put us in the cattle car, and ship us to Plaszow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: P-L-S-Z-L-O-S-Z, Plaszow.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was that? In what--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Poland. And when we arrived there, Poland-- I just found out. It wasn't a-- it, it wasn't a ghetto for them. It was a Polish concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were there with Edith, your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: And all five of us, all the five cousins-- Weiss girls and my sister,

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: --all the way.

speaker: interviewer
text: --what was Plaszow like? And what did you do there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Plaszow, when we arrived there, our amazement was that the Polish people were dressed normally-- western clothes, boots on girls, beautiful hair. And they look normal. And we arrive, we're looking like-- [SIGHS] like caricatures, you know? Crazy.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we were the first Hungarian-- we found that out after that-- to be transferred to, to Plaszow, which was all Polish people till then. So [SIGHS] when we arrived there, they ask if you arbeiten, what can-- you know, what can you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my sister, who hated sewing all her life, she volunteers. And she says, I sew. And so does my other three cousins. No, two cousin, who really were dressmakers, they say that their arbeiten is Schneider, schneidering. So they are sewers.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they get separated. I mean, we're in a camp, but in the same barrack. And they put them in a different direction. They were working in a munition factory, making soldier's uniform.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the one who didn't have trade, like me and my oldest cousin-- she was the oldest. That time she was about 26. But it was at my height, so she always stayed front of me. So-- no, behind me, because my sister was the tallest. She was front, then I was behind her. And that's how we were lined up, always the same way. And we were assigned to work outside that ghetto, where they had the German soldiers building barns for the horses. And we were assigned to wheelbarrow the cement for the bricklayers. And at 15, I was doing this. At one-- OK, and things, as, as she started working, she would the next day, right away, they steal, in between their clothes, a little piece of cloth for a kerchief. So she would bring out a, a babushka to cover my bare head. And the next day, maybe something else. They always try to steal something out of the-- what you may call the shop. And their life was normal. They got up. They got-- we got decent food in there, and a piece of bread, and margarine with that, and something. And we would come back to the barrack, 6 o'clock or so. At one point, we are doing this, and everything is in a-- normal as can be. We separate each other every morning. She goes to work. And we go with the other transport, and we go through this gate from Plaszow, which you saw it in a Schindler story. And we go out of that gate to where the German soldiers have the barns for the horses. And it was-- I wasn't actually near Krakau, where we were. And we were marched, always walking. And one day-- this is already-- we're in there in June. And it's very, very warm. And they give us, I think, a half-an-hour rest period, where we ate, like, lunch. It was so hot that we sort of-- us go behind a wagon. And that's where we settled down. And that's where we're sitting. And we didn't get up. Exact a half hour, we just sat. I guess everybody was tired. And we-- and we just sat. And all of a sudden, we had a terrible, terrible German soldier who was watching us with two dogs constantly. And that soldier was evidently-- the rumor was that he was a killer. And they took him out of jail, just, you know, to be a soldier watching the Jews. At that point, he takes us-- we were about 10 girls. Not all of them were from the same barrack. My cousin, of course, me-- against the wall of the barn, which is like-- just the wall was up. But the whole barn wasn't completely built-- a roof or anything, because they were just bricked. And lines up on there. And we get 25 lashes with a rubber hose. And as a little schlimazel, I'm in a end-- [POINTS] towards the end, where is no place to move.

speaker: interviewer
text: So as he hitting, everybody jumps from the pain. And, you know, to, to, to-- maybe he would miss us. I had no place to jump. I get 25 lashes at the corner of the wall. Then they take us to the jail, to the bunker-- solitary, [POINTS] this size.

speaker: interviewee
text: That day, [SIGHS] the girls don't go back to the barrack. The girls, my cousins, my sister, gets back, and two of us, of course, missing. And the rumor is that they put us in jail. And my sister, just probably sure that she'll never see us again. They kept us overnight there with the rats. My cousin is next to me, tiny, [POINTS] just about, like, 5 by 10, just her nose to be out. Scary, dark, everything, crying-- and I, you know, and, and she's-- I think she's praying. And she says to me all night, talk to me, Evika. Talk to me, Evika, so we know we're alive. Talk to me, Evika. And of-- you don't sleep. You just stand. Or you-- finally, when you couldn't stand anymore, you scoot down. But that was just completely a square box, [POINTS] dark and everything. In a morning-- [SIGHS] and we're in pain. You couldn't even feel the pain because you don't know if you're scared, or they going to shoot you, or what's going to be the end-- for that reason. And next day, finally, they let us out. And we get back to the barrack. And when my sister-- there was-- she was ready to go to work. And she won't go. And the, the, the girl-- there was a Polish girl always who was already previously there a longer time, was watching. And she was assigned to watch the whole barrack and all the girls. Finally, she feels sorry. And she lets my sister be out, that she's going to report krank for her. And when my sister sees my back-- this thick, [POINTS] 25 lashes, rubber hose, showing on my back, all day she was putting cold compress on it, everything else. And it was unreal. It was just unreal. You survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in Plaszow? And where did you go from there?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Plaszow, we were about-- till middle of July.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this is July, 1945.

speaker: interviewee
text: 1945. And then again--

speaker: interviewee
text: '44. '44. And that was the first year, yeah. '44, July. And the bombing was tremendous. The Russians were, were coming towards that area-- Plaszow, Krakau. And they were bombing. And at one point, I volunteered to work in a kitchen, so I get double food. And I have no idea how my sister let me do that. And I did. And I came home, like, 11 o'clock to the barrack, which was only a few-- maybe a block to walk. And I come one night. And it's lit up. And it just shines-- and bombing. And I wasn't scared. I just walk towards the barrack. And double portion of food was in my hand, and some soup or some bread. I don't even know what. And-- but, but noise and the sound was so tremendous that my sister did not sleep. And, of course, after that, she didn't let me go back to the kitchen that, you know, she let me alone. And the bombing was so bad that they had to empty the camp-- the Plaszow, the concentration camp. And then they shipped us back to Auschwitz. Again, we going. But, you know, we are getting back to the cattle car. We don't know where we going. That's one thing. We didn't know. But when we arrived, we knew. And when we arrived in July and got off the cattle car, the smell was so intense, that's when we realized. We did-- I mean, we really actually didn't see the crematorium or smell that till then. Because in Krakau, there was selection in there, too. But they shot you. They shot you. They shot you anytime they feel like to. They counted you. And if you were, I don't know-- they decide every tenth is going to be-- get out of the line, and they shoot you. They just shot you right there. But we didn't see no crematorium. Or we didn't see the mass grave or nothing because it was in front of us. But they were very, very cruel-- very cruel. And when we get back to Auschwitz, then we feel, this is it. Sure enough, again, we going to go through. Didn't shave us that time because we still didn't have no hair anyway. But we going to the shower. And when we went back to that shower, by that time, you already heard enough stories. And you were aware that this is it. You're not going to come out. And when you did come out, there was a bigger shock that you're really alive-- and they just showered you. And they give us some other junk. And we were assigned to C Lager. And [SIGHS] Auschwitz-- actually, our function was-- that time, it was getting up, 5 o'clock, raus. They would keep us outside-- rain, shine, and cool morning-- three, four hours. And every day, they count us. They were very good of keeping records, the German. They had to count us. If one was missing out of line-- like I told you, formation of five-- and one may be sick, or, or left in a barrack, or really escaped, or something, or died, you would be punished, a whole barrack. And we would be outside for hours and hours and hours. And then we learned what Auschwitz like. And we saw the barbed wires, you know, electrical. We even witnessed two girls get electrocuted because they see their cousins, or somebody, relation. They see that they're just marching into the other camp through the wires. And they're not even thinking, and they're running like this. [POINTS] And in that second, they were electrocuted.
